narrative: As an Asian male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.70—well above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model ultimately predicted a 47% chance of failing the bar exam on your first try, tipping the scales toward rejection. This prediction draws from patterns in data on thousands of students' academic records, family backgrounds, and exam outcomes, aiming to identify those likely to succeed as lawyers. The model's decision hinged on just a few key factors from your profile, with your undergraduate GPA emerging as the strongest positive influence, as it signaled stronger preparation than most applicants and pushed the prediction toward expecting you to pass. However, your LSAT score of 27—noticeably below the average of 36.7—had the single largest negative pull, outweighing that strength by making the model worry about your readiness for the rigors of law school and the bar. Your third-year high school decile ranking of 3, lower than the typical 5.53, added further concern, suggesting a dip in performance that echoed patterns among students who later struggled on the exam. Compared to typical students, where full-time enrollment is the norm at over 92%, your planned full-time status didn't sway things much, nor did your middle-income family background. These top influences—your LSAT and year-three grades pulling toward failure, countered somewhat by your college GPA—created an overall risk profile that didn't meet the threshold for approval. It's understandable to feel frustrated, especially with your above-average undergrad record as an Asian male navigating competitive admissions. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to or above average, as it carries heavy weight in these predictions. Strengthening recent academic showings, perhaps through additional coursework or a post-bac program, could also help reframe your year-three decile. Gathering stronger letters of recommendation or relevant experience might provide context in a future application. Finally, appealing directly to the admissions office with this explanation in hand could open doors—many schools value persistence and self-awareness in applicants like you.
